speaker: interviewer
text: This is Tape 3 of our interview with Magda Blau. Magda, you were talking about collecting the food from the kitchen and asking others to help you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about collecting food from the kitchen--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and asking others to-- to help you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you go on with that story, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. The first time when we went to fetch tea, the German woman didn't want to give us, because they said you are competition to us. You are prostitutes. You are taking our jobs.

speaker: interviewee
text: So definitely none of those girls are prostitutes. They was really the best material. They're teachers, doctors, nurses, because this generation, the young ones-- anyway, so I said, no. We are not competition to you. We don't want anything to harm you. But please, let us take the tea. Oh, but you are so spoiled. You won't to be able to carry that tea. So I arranged so many that to every kettle four. But I said, is there somebody who is willing to take the kettle with me? So one girl [INAUDIBLE]. So instead four, we went two.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we went I said, now you run, because I will be running. I-- I was very sport-minded at home. I did lot of sport. And I was a real strong girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I could take the kettle which had I don't know how many liters, I think 60 liters. But I said, you run. And then she saw that I was running. She was not so [INAUDIBLE]. I said run, because I want you to remember let us to have that-- the food and drink next time. So she-- from this moment, she had respect for-- for me. And then I came with my people, fetched the food or like the soup or-- so no trouble anymore. So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I tried to divide the food evenly. I have a very good memory for faces, so I-- and I am straight away saying it, you know? I said, girls, there is food for everybody. This was while you were still with 600 in the one area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. I said, there is food for everybody. But it's for everybody just one portion. If somebody comes for twice, then somebody won't-- won't have it, so please don't come twice. I will recognize you anyway. And that wouldn't be nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: So by the way, as I talked to people-- so it was good. Then I said to the girls who was working like in the [INAUDIBLE]-- like hospital, but it was called [INAUDIBLE]-- steal as much medication as you can, and specially for bruises and for diarrhea, and every possible medication you can. Steal because I am sure we will need it. And to the girls who was working in the-- where they-- the sewing room, so I said, cut in pieces like a sheet so I would have for bandages. And that was a clever way of thinking, because when the girls came home-- so the German woman was right. She said, how long you think they will last, or you would last? They've been all bruised, sick. So I straight away started to put some ointment and bandages, and started to gave them coal or medication for diarrhea. Whatever I could, I did. And so much so that I been so busy with that that I didn't feel hunger. And they were the girls that went outside to work, in the-- in agriculture.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they worked outside. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of jobs were they doing out there?

speaker: interviewee
text: You wouldn't believe it what the Germans are. They was carrying rocks from one place to the other, backwards and forwards. And they had to do it in running, rush, rush, rush, rush.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they did demolishing things, because prior of that there was villages. So how did they do the demolishing? They put a group of girls up and a group of girl down. And there, the girls up, they had to throw the bricks down. And often the-- the bricks fell on the girls downstair. How did you know that's what they did? Did you see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But they told us when they came home. They said-- because I said, oh, what happened to you? No. They-- she said, that what happened to me. And similar, there was certain things that they really did, like they digged all the potatoes, which the peasants put in. Some things they did. But most of what it was not for real productive, but for distracting people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what sort of work at this stage were you doing at the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I did. I fixed the bed, because I wasn't the only one. There, because it's for 600 people, so it was a few girls. This German woman picked a few. One of them at one stage lived in [INAUDIBLE] too. And so we fixed the bed. We went to early morning for bringing in tea, dividing the tea. Then we fixed the bed, cleaned the floor. Then we went to fetch the bread. They will cut the bread [INAUDIBLE]. And we fetched the margarine, or whatever it was. And then we went to fetch the soup. And when that came home, so we divided. So it's a lot of from one to the other. It was always busy. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you do this for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now we've been there 'til August--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of?

speaker: interviewee
text: '42. And then all the woman was transferred to Birkenau. When Auschwitz was a hell, Birkenau was definitely the biggest hell what you can imagine, because there, our part of living, that used to be for horses. They call it stables? Yeah. And there was a place for one horse. They made the room for 30 woman. They made two planks, 10, 10, and 10 was on the floor. 30 people was living there. Not enough blankets. Not enough dress. Rain or shine, you been always in the same thing. So imagine how that was worn down, but they didn't get anything new.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened at Auschwitz to-- to make it so that you had to leave where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they filled the Auschwitz with men, because it was-- one part was already men's camp. And then on the place of woman, they fill that in with men and we went to Birkenau. Birkenau was a terrible place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any women left at--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any women left at the main Auschwitz camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took all the women.

speaker: interviewee
text: All [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did they take you? How far was it from one site to the other site?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we walked. It was an hour walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: An hour's walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] They supposed to have canalization, but they didn't finish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you explain what that means?

speaker: interviewee
text: Water pipes. They dig out to put water pipes in it to get water for the washrooms and for drinking water. But they didn't finish it. It was only in work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for drinking water was one-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how you call it? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No. I don't know if you know in old-fashioned place where is deep inside is water. And they bring the water up with-- Like a well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Water well. So it was one. And that was for thousands of woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many thousand were there? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: It could be there 10,000. Later it was even more.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were brought across in about August.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the weather like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The weather was still warm. And they came thirsty. So what happened the girls was-- when-- when the water came up, then hundred of woman wanted to-- so what happened, that lot of woman fell in and they drowned. And later, they made-- they finished up, so it was a washroom. And the toilet it was just a-- a hole. And there was a piece of plank. And now you are sitting on the plank. If you lost balance, you fell in. And if you fell in, that was your finish.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a plank with the sewer straight underneath.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just a hole, a cement hole.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big would the hole have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The hole was as-- in the hallways-- in the hallways of the room. It could be a few meters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And deep?

speaker: interviewee
text: And deep a few meters. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember one girl fell in. And she climbed out. But as she was, with the shit over, she had to march through the camp. And there they took her to the crematorium. They didn't let her wash herself.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw her marching.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you know she was taken to the crematorium?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because they said. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, until-- 'til the holes was not finished, so weak people fell in. And it always was a whistle that you had to run in, because they was afraid [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] aeroplanes went through, they shouldn't see the people. So people had to run in. A lot-- a lot of people fell in in that-- how you say it?-- in that which wasn't filled yet with-- so in the hole.

speaker: interviewer
text: The well or the sewer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: The well or the sewer?

speaker: interviewee
text: The sewer, where the-- where the pipes should be. And-- and when the whistle came, everybody had to run in. If somebody was weak, you couldn't jump over. You fell in. You fell in, the whistle came, and you've been in so they took you straight away to crematorium. And often I ran out quickly and I pulled the girls out and took them in the barrack. Once I-- I was putting a girl in. The whistle came and I was there, so I was taking these-- all those girls on the truck to go to the crematorium. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But you see, this is unfortune-- unbelievables. A woman, that woman who first didn't let us have the tea from beginning of camp, she wanted to be very friendly with me. But this time, those girls liked very much girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said that we don't know that and I hope you-- she doesn't mind. We still can be friends. But we didn't have any contact.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when I was on that truck going to the crematorium, she just passed by, because she was preparing a commando to go out to some work. When she saw me there, she recognized me. And so there was SS man watching.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she said to him, that's the girl I need. I can't walk out with my commando, because she is missing. So she gave me two slaps, pulls me down from there, and-- and complains to the SS man loading up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we went a few steps and she said, and now disappear. And with that, she saved my life. Things like that happens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the work you did once you came to Birkenau.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to Birkenau, I still was a helper, like the same thing. And then when I met Grese, Irma Grese, the Angel of Death-- I went with the girls for-- to fetch our bread. And as usually, I talked to people. I said, I am sorry that you have to come. I know you come from work and you must be tired and worn. But we need the bread. I can't bring my-- the bread for the thousand people. This time we've been thousand in a barrack. So-- but I-- I will myself carry the bread. Irma Grese was there in charge of the that broad bread department.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember what date this was, around--

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the date of this-- at this time? Was it-- what year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was-- it was about October.

speaker: interviewee
text: After we been there-- maybe it was end of September, beginning of October. I mean, who counted the days? We didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, so she-- she was watching me. And she said, come here. What's your name? And I said Magda. And-- and I forgot myself that she's SS woman, because she was a chubby girl with long plaited blonde hair until here. So somehow I said, and what's yours? And she said Irma. And I said, nice to know you. And with that thing, I established myself as certain respect with her, that I as a prisoner ask her what's your name and told her nice to know you. [LAUGHS] When I think back, it-- it's silly. But for her, it was that probably the way they explained that the Jews are very weaklings or how you say the other? I'm not sure what word you want.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like, you know, they are afraid to-- to speak up. So-- and here is somebody who not only spoke up, but she spoke up and she asked her name. I asked the name of SS woman and tell her-- so anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that-- that what I did for a while, making the bed, the same thing, the cleaning up, helping. And-- and making sure that the people are out for the-- for roll call. Tell me about roll call. In Birkenau, what would happen for roll call?

speaker: interviewee
text: Roll call was a terrible thing, because people came so tired home and so uncomfortable was the sleeping and they was sick and weak. So they-- in the morning, they felt not to get up from bed. But the appell had to stand. And the counting had to be done, because still the counting wasn't done until the number didn't-- being perfect. 'Til then, the people had to stand.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had to say, come on. We must go out. We have to be counted, so-- and then they let us stand for hours.

speaker: interviewee
text: Imagine first it was cold. Then it was warn. Then it was tiring. But it was-- it took us always hours. Now and then, again, bringing the bread, cutting the bread, cutting the butter, cutting everything. Then bringing--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any-- Did anyone-- did anyone ever complain to you about the portion they were given or-- or was that something that you didn't ever experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You see, you are-- you are so below low low, that you-- when you get this little bread, you don't complain. It's too much. But you are so happy to have it. So you're eating by drops and drops. It should last. And maybe they eat just the half and the half later and half later. It-- it is-- and this bread wasn't really bread. It was lot of sawdust. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But still, it was a-- a little flour in it too. And-- and you could bite in it too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any way that people could get extra food at Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes a commando, when it worked longer hours or harder work, so maybe they got one portion of soup more or one portion of bread more. But it has to be something something if they got it. Otherwise, they just got that what they have. And the saying was that you can survive three months on this food. And then you are weaker and you die, if they don't take you sooner to the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there 3 and half years by miracles.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So what happened at that point? You were still the assistant.

speaker: interviewee
text: Assistant. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: First, I got sick. Typhus were raging. So I got typhus, spotted typhus and paratyphus, which went with very high fever. My tongue was so swollen that it didn't fit in my mouth. And it was so wrinkled-- like when you open the stomach of a chicken inside and it's so-- so was my tongue.

speaker: interviewee
text: My temperature was very high. And I was talking like hallucinating in the high temperature. And what I wanted is tomatoes. Where to get tomatoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: But the girls was like sisters. Some was working in a place, Raisko. And they cultivated vegetable for the SS. So they steal a-- a tomato. They put it in her-- they've been so flat, you didn't see it. If they'd been lucky that nobody looked through, so she went through. But if they found it, you went to the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So someone was risking their life to bring you--

speaker: interviewee
text: They're risking their life. They bring the tomato. And with that, I rubbed my tongue, so-- you know, because it soothe it a little bit. Anyway, the same thing was I wanted [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] lemonade. A girl was working in the SS canteen. She again on her-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like she risked her life. And she-- she steal a bottle of lemonade and she brought it to me, so that's what I was living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember that girl's name that did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember the girl's name that brought you the lemonade?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was from our-- our town. I don't know if she was Helen or-- can't. There were so many names, can't remember. But I know she did that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened during appell when you were sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. First of all, it was that-- it was the danger that the SS man by the name [? Shtibitz. ?] He was a big sadist, and he couldn't-- couldn't quicker and quicker get the people to the gas chamber. So-- and we couldn't walk and couldn't get up, we've been so weak. So we stayed in bed. So but--

speaker: interviewer
text: There was more than just you sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: 30 girls was-- all had typhoid and they all was in bed. But we thought that during day, nobody will come in. But in any case, some girls who was close to me, whom I helped all the time-- that's how it is, one helps the other. She said to one of the girls who-- who was also from our group, you do the night shift for Magda and she will say that she's night shift. And I will steal some potatoes from the kitchen and I will cooks for you and so-- and that will help you-- for that work what you did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the work? Because the toilets was very far and they didn't-- and it was very dark and didn't let people go there, so there was wheelbarrows and people finished themself in the wheelbarrow. When the wheelbarrow was full, they took it early in the morning to the latrine and they spilled it over and so on. That was her duty. So the SS man came. And when he came to me, so I quickly-- oh, so quick like I don't know how could I manage. I said, I am night shift, but if you want I jump out from bed and I will do my duty.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, shut up. And that minute saved my life. The others didn't have that excuse, so all the 29 girls went to the gas chambers. In a few days, they will be all right. But what happened with me, I had-- because I had that paratyphus, so I lost my vision. I couldn't hear. I couldn't walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they just lifted me up like sack of potatoes and I dropped me wherever. Sing I could, so I was always singing. I had a good voice then. And if somebody came, they just kicked me, so I knew be quiet. That means be quiet.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so in charge of this barrack was a German woman, non-Jewish German woman. And she said, I can't keep her. She's not good for anything. I sent her to the gas chambers, because otherwise I am responsible.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one of the girls from the group says, listen. I can sew. You bring me thread and needle, I'm going to sew you the nicest thing what you want. And also, if you bring me some, I will cook you the best thing, what you want.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just leave Magda, because in a few days she will be all right. Leave her. If something happen, I will be responsible. And that's how--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you-- can you remember her name, the person that said all that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who did that? Ruzjenca was her name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the girl who was doing night duty, that was [? Sarin. ?]
